perturbation.original_text: LICENSEE AGREES THAT LICENSOR WOULD BE IRREPARABLY HARMED BY ANY BREACH OF THIS AGREEMENT BY LICENSEE THAT HARMS THE BRAND, AND THAT LICENSOR MAY (IN ADDITION TO ITS OTHER RIGHTS AND REMEDIES HEREIN) SEEK TEMPORARY, PRELIMINARY OR PERMANENT INJUNCTIVE RELIEF (INCLUDING SPECIFIC PERFORMANCE) TO ENJOIN OR PREVENT ANY SUCH BREACH, WITHOUT POSTING BOND OR OTHER SECURITY.
perturbation.changed_text: LICENSEE AGREES THAT LICENSOR MAY (IN ADDITION TO ITS OTHER RIGHTS AND REMEDIES HEREIN) SEEK TEMPORARY, PRELIMINARY OR PERMANENT INJUNCTIVE RELIEF TO ENJOIN OR PREVENT ANY SUCH BREACH, BUT ONLY UPON POSTING A BOND OR OTHER SECURITY.
perturbation.explanation: By requiring the Licensor to post a bond or security before obtaining injunctive relief, the contract deviates from standard trademark law practice and potentially applicable equitable principles, which often allow for injunctive relief without bond in cases of clear trademark infringement where irreparable harm is demonstrated. This introduces risk as courts in New York, where this agreement is governed, may grant injunctions without requiring a bond, especially when the harm is evident. This can be seen as an attempt to limit the Licensor's remedies in a way that contradicts established legal precedents and equitable principles.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New York Civil Practice Law and Rules (CPLR) § 6312
perturbation.law_explanation: CPLR § 6312(b) generally requires an undertaking (bond) for preliminary injunctions, but courts retain discretion, particularly in cases involving clear rights and minimal risk to the enjoined party. The modified clause mandates a bond, removing the court's discretion and contradicting the flexibility allowed under New York law in certain circumstances, especially where irreparable harm is evident in trademark cases.

Motion papers; undertaking; issues of fact. (a) Affidavit; other evidence. On a motion for a preliminary injunction the plaintiff shall show, by affidavit and such other evidence as may be submitted, that there is a cause of action, and either that the defendant threatens or is about to do, or is doing or procuring or suffering to be done, an act in violation of the plaintiff's rights respecting the subject of the action and tending to render the judgment ineffectual; or that the plaintiff has demanded and would be entitled to a judgment restraining the defendant from the commission or continuance of an act, which, if committed or continued during the pendency of the action, would produce injury to the plaintiff. (b) Undertaking. Except as provided in section 2512 and in actions brought under section two hundred sixty-five-a of the real property law, prior to the granting of a preliminary injunction, the plaintiff shall give an undertaking in an amount to be fixed by the court, that the plaintiff, if it is finally determined that he or she was not entitled to an injunction, will pay to the defendant all damages and costs which may be sustained by reason of the injunction, including: 1. if the injunction is to stay proceedings in another action, on any ground other than that a report, verdict or decision was obtained by actual fraud, all damages and costs which may be, or which have been, awarded in the other action to the defendant as well as all damages and costs which may be awarded him or her in the action in which the injunction was granted; or, 2. if the injunction is to stay proceedings in an action to recover real property, or for dower, on any ground other than that a verdict, report or decision was obtained by actual fraud, all damages and costs which may be, or which have been, awarded to the defendant in the action in which the injunction was gran
perturbation.justification: The original contract allows the licensor to seek injunctive relief without posting a bond, which is standard practice in trademark infringement cases. The changed text mandates a bond, removing the court's discretion. CPLR § 6312(b), as indicated in Scraped Law Snippet 1, generally requires an undertaking for preliminary injunctions, but courts retain discretion. The modification contradicts the flexibility allowed under New York law, especially where irreparable harm is evident in trademark cases.

perturbation.original_text: EACH PARTY HEREBY IRREVOCABLY AND UNCONDITIONALLY WAIVES TO THE FULLEST EXTENT PERMITTED BY APPLICABLE LAW, ANY RIGHT SUCH PARTY MAY HAVE TO A TRIAL BY JURY IN RESPECT TO ANY ACTION RELATING TO OR ARISING OUT OF THIS AGREEMENT.
perturbation.changed_text: LICENSEE HEREBY WAIVES ANY RIGHT SUCH PARTY MAY HAVE TO A TRIAL BY JURY IN RESPECT TO ANY ACTION RELATING TO OR ARISING OUT OF THIS AGREEMENT.
perturbation.explanation: By removing 'EACH PARTY' and replacing it with 'LICENSEE', the contract now only waives the licensee's right to a jury trial but not the licensor's. While parties can waive jury trials, the waiver must be knowing and voluntary. In New York, such waivers are generally enforceable, but this modification creates an imbalance that could be challenged, particularly if the Licensor seeks a jury trial. This can introduce legal risk if the Licensor later argues that the waiver is unenforceable against them, potentially invalidating the waiver altogether.
perturbation.law_explanation: While Article I, § 2 of the New York Constitution guarantees the right to a jury trial, it also permits waivers in civil cases. However, such waivers must be clear, knowing, and voluntary. By only having the LICENSEE waive their right to a jury trial, this contract implies that the LICENSOR retains that right, creating an uneven playing field. Although waivers are permitted, a court might scrutinize this one-sided waiver more closely, especially if the party that didn't waive the right now seeks a jury trial, arguing that the original intent was for a mutual waiver or if the wording is deemed ambiguous.
perturbation.justification: The modification removes the waiver of jury trial rights for the licensor, creating an imbalance in the contract. This contradicts the principle that waivers of such rights should be knowing and voluntary for all parties involved. The original contract explicitly waived the right for 'EACH PARTY,' while the modified version only waives the right for the 'LICENSEE.' The absence of a similar waiver for the licensor implies they retain the right, potentially undermining the enforceability of the waiver as a whole.
